title: Amazon Product Catalog — System Design
description: Design the catalog behind a large e-commerce store: browse and search hundreds of millions of products, filter by facets, and render fast product pages — while inventory and prices change constantly. The themes are the read/write (CQRS) split, search + faceting, a denormalized read model, and keeping inventory/price consistent across that split.

nodes.label: Shopper Client
nodes.description: Browses, searches, filters, and views product detail pages.

nodes.label: Catalog Service
nodes.description: Owns product write model: create/update product records.

nodes.label: Catalog Store
nodes.description: Authoritative product data, sharded by product id.

nodes.label: Product Read Model
nodes.description: Denormalized product view assembled for fast detail-page reads.

nodes.label: Search / Facet Index
nodes.description: Inverted index + facets for search and filtering.

nodes.label: Search Service
nodes.description: Resolves queries/filters to product candidates and ranks them.

nodes.label: Inventory Service
nodes.description: Owns authoritative stock levels per item/warehouse.

nodes.label: Pricing Service
nodes.description: Owns prices/promotions; changes frequently and independently.

nodes.label: Catalog Change Stream
nodes.description: Product/price/inventory change events feeding read model + index.

nodes.label: Indexer / Projector
nodes.description: Builds the read model and search index from change events.

functional: Search products by keyword and filter by facets (category, brand, price, rating).
Render a product detail page (description, images, price, availability, reviews summary).
Show current price and availability accurately enough to shop.
List products by category with sorting (relevance, price, rating).
nonFunctional: Extremely read-heavy: browse/search reads dwarf writes.
Low read latency (search and detail p99 < 200 ms).
Catalog content (descriptions) can be eventually consistent after a seller edit (seconds).
Price and availability must be fresh enough to trust at the point of purchase.
Scale to hundreds of millions of products and high query volume.

capacity.label: Price changes
capacity.note: Promotions, repricing; independent of catalog edits.

api.description: Search and filter products; returns ranked results with facet counts.
Get the product detail view; availability/price fetched live for purchase accuracy.
Seller creates/updates a product; emits a change event projected into read model + index.

dataModel.name: inventory
dataModel.note: Authoritative stock, owned by the inventory service.

patterns.name: CQRS (read/write split)
patterns.what: Separate the normalized write model from denormalized read models built by projection.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy domains where read and write shapes differ greatly.

patterns.name: Search index + faceting
patterns.what: An inverted index with facet aggregations for keyword search and filtering.

patterns.name: Denormalized read model
patterns.what: Precompute a render-ready product view so detail pages are a single lookup.
patterns.whenToUse: Hot detail pages assembled from many sources.

patterns.name: Change-stream projection
patterns.what: Project write/price/inventory changes into read model and index via an event stream.
patterns.whenToUse: Keeping derived read stores eventually consistent with sources.

patterns.name: Authoritative live read for volatile data
patterns.what: Read fast-changing data (stock, price) live from its owner at decision time, not from the cached view.
patterns.whenToUse: When staleness on a value would cause a wrong purchase.

patterns.name: Service ownership (bounded contexts)
patterns.what: Catalog, pricing, and inventory are separate services owning their data.

steps.title: 1. Split Reads from Writes (CQRS)
description: The shapes differ sharply: writes are normalized product records sellers edit occasionally; reads are denormalized, render-ready views served at massive volume. Forcing both onto one model makes neither fast.

Separate them. The catalog service owns the authoritative normalized write model; read-optimized stores (a denormalized read model + a search index) are derived from it. Writes update the source of truth; reads never touch it directly.
This read/write split (CQRS) is the architectural backbone — it lets the read side scale and be shaped independently of the write side.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why not serve product pages and search directly from the normalized product table?

concepts.definition: Separate command (write) and query (read) models, with reads projected from writes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets the read side be denormalized, indexed, and scaled independently of the write side.
concepts.example: Normalized products table for sellers; denormalized read model + search index for shoppers.

whyNow: The read/write split frames everything; stating it first is what lets search, read models, and projection make sense as derived stores.

recap.after: Authoritative write model separate from derived read stores.
recap.newRisk: Search/browse over hundreds of millions of products needs a real index, not table scans.

steps.title: 2. Search and Faceting
description: Browse and search are the primary entry points. Build an inverted index over product text and attributes; serve keyword queries and rank results (relevance, popularity, rating).

Faceting is the e-commerce twist: alongside results, return counts per filter value (brand, price range, rating) so shoppers can narrow down. The index computes these aggregations as part of the query.
Shard the index by product; replicate for query volume. Hydrate result cards from the read model (title, image, price) rather than the index itself.
steps.decisionPrompt: Besides matching results, what does an e-commerce search need to return that a web search doesn't?

concepts.definition: Returning filter values with result counts alongside the matches.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets shoppers narrow large result sets — core to product discovery.

whyNow: Search + faceting is how shoppers actually find products; it's the dominant read path and the e-commerce-specific depth.

recap.after: Keyword + faceted search over a sharded index.
recap.newRisk: Result/detail rendering pulls from many sources — too slow if assembled per request.

steps.title: 3. Denormalized Product Read Model
description: A product page combines data from many places: catalog attributes, images, rating summary, category info. Assembling that per request (fan-out joins) is slow at this scale.

Precompute a denormalized, render-ready product view keyed by product id — a single lookup serves the detail page. The read model carries the source version it reflects, so it's verifiable and rebuildable.
Search hydrates result cards from the same read model. It's derived state: a cache loss is a rebuild from the write model, not data loss.
steps.decisionPrompt: A product page needs data from 5 services. How do you render it in one fast lookup?
whyNow: The denormalized read model is how the detail-page latency target is met; tying it to a source version keeps it trustworthy.

concepts.definition: A precomputed, render-ready view assembled from multiple sources.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a multi-source fan-out into a single fast read.
concepts.example: product_view[p_9] = {title, images, attrs, rating} ready to render.

recap.newRisk: The read model lags the write model — how and how fast does it update?

steps.title: 4. Keep Read Stores Fresh via Change Streams
description: When a seller edits a product (or category/rating changes), the read model and search index must update. The catalog service emits change events to a stream; an indexer/projector consumes them and updates the read model and index.

This keeps the read side eventually consistent with the write side — acceptable for catalog content (a description edit appearing in seconds). The change stream is the single mechanism feeding all derived stores.
Version every projection by the source write version so you can detect lag and rebuild deterministically.
steps.decisionPrompt: A seller fixes a typo in a title. How does it reach search results and the product page?
whyNow: Projection is what keeps the derived read stores correct; making it the single change-stream-driven path is the clean CQRS completion.

concepts.definition: Deriving and updating read stores from a stream of write-model changes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps multiple read shapes consistent from one source of change.
concepts.example: Product change -> stream -> projector updates read model + search index.

recap.after: Read model + index updated from a change stream; rebuildable.
recap.newRisk: Price and stock change far more often and being stale on them causes wrong purchases.

steps.title: 5. Inventory and Price: Freshness Where It Matters
description: Catalog content tolerating seconds of staleness is fine. Price and availability do not: showing an out-of-stock item as buyable, or a stale price at checkout, is a real defect.

Treat inventory and pricing as separate services owning their authoritative data. For volatile values, read them live from their owner at decision time (add-to-cart, checkout) rather than trusting a projected snapshot. The read model can show an approximate badge ('in stock') but the purchase path verifies live.
This splits consistency by data type: eventually-consistent catalog content via projection, authoritative live reads for money/stock.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which product fields can be eventually consistent, and which must be read live at purchase time?

concepts.definition: Different freshness guarantees for content vs volatile values (stock/price).
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids both slow reads (over-consistency) and wrong purchases (over-staleness).
concepts.example: Title/desc: projected, eventually consistent. Stock/price at checkout: live from owner.

patterns: Authoritative live read for volatile data
whyNow: Mixing eventual content with authoritative volatile reads is the key e-commerce correctness insight and the most common senior follow-up.

recap.after: Eventual for content, authoritative-live for stock/price.
recap.newRisk: Popular products and a hot category create read hotspots; projection can lag under bursts.

traps.trap: Serving stock/price from the cached read model.
traps.why: A stale 'in stock' or old price leads to failed/incorrect purchases.
traps.instead: Read volatile values live from their owning service at the purchase decision.

steps.title: 6. Scaling Reads, Hotspots, and Rebuilds
description: Reads dominate, so scale them: shard catalog/index by product, replicate read replicas and search shards, and cache hot product views and popular search results at the edge.

Handle hotspots: a viral product or a hot category gets disproportionate reads — already absorbed by the read model + caches, with replicas for the index shard. Projection bursts (e.g. a bulk catalog import) are buffered by the change stream and consumed by scalable projectors.
Because all read stores are derived, they're rebuildable: a corrupted index or read model is reprojected from the authoritative write model and change log.
steps.decisionPrompt: A deal makes one product go viral. What absorbs the read spike, and what stays authoritative?
whyNow: Closing on scaling/hotspots/rebuilds shows the derived-store design is operable and recoverable, completing the CQRS story.

recap.after: Sharded, replicated, cached reads; rebuildable derived stores.
recap.newRisk: Projection lag under bulk imports and cache staleness — bounded by scalable projectors and live reads for volatile data.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Edge cache + read replicas + index shard replication.
Change stream buffers; autoscale projectors; prioritize live-shopper-visible fields.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Rebuild it by reprojecting from the write model / change log; reads degrade gracefully meanwhile.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat index + read model as rebuildable derived state.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — CQRS Product Catalog
finalDesign.description: The catalog service owns the normalized write model; a change stream projects edits into a denormalized product read model and a sharded search/facet index (eventually consistent, rebuildable). Shoppers search/filter via the index and hydrate result cards and detail pages from the read model in a single lookup. Inventory and pricing are separate services owning authoritative data; volatile stock/price are read live from them at purchase-decision time while content stays projected. Reads are sharded, replicated, and edge-cached; projection bursts buffer in the stream; all derived stores rebuild from the authoritative write model.

functional.how: Index queries with sort over the read model.

nonFunctional.how: Authoritative live reads at purchase time.
Sharded write model, index, and read replicas.

say: Confirm extremely read-heavy browse/search with seller writes.
Pin low read latency; agree content can be eventually consistent.
Flag that price/availability need to be trustworthy at purchase.
Search + faceting over an inverted index.
Denormalized read model for fast pages.
Change-stream projection keeps read stores fresh.
Consistency by data type: live reads for stock/price.
Scaling reads, hotspots, and rebuildable derived stores.
Tradeoffs: projection lag, eventual content vs live volatile reads, index sharding.
Mention recommendations and reviews as adjacent systems.

expectations: Uses a search index and a product table.
May miss CQRS, projection, and stock/price consistency.
Designs CQRS with projected read model + faceted search.
Keeps read stores fresh via change streams.
Designs rebuildable derived stores and projection-burst handling.
Discusses hotspot mitigation and index sharding tradeoffs.

followUps: How do you keep search results consistent with a just-edited product?
How would you add personalized ranking/recommendations to search?
How do you handle a product sold by many sellers (offers/buy-box)?
How would you support near-real-time price changes across millions of products?
How do you prevent showing out-of-stock items as available?
